abstract: Developing lead-free relaxors that simultaneously exhibit high capacitive energy density ($W_{rec}$) and superior storage efficiency ($\eta$) remains a critical challenge for advancing pulsed power technologies. While local compositional heterogeneity is known to drive the formation of complex polar structures in solid solution relaxors, its specific role in optimizing energy storage performance is frequently underestimated. In this study, we demonstrate that a remarkable $W_{rec}$ of 15.2 J cm⁻³ and an ultrahigh $\eta$ of 91% can be achieved by engineering local chemical clustering within $Bi_{0.5}Na_{0.5}TiO_3$-$BaTiO_3$-based relaxors. Utilizing a three-dimensional atomistic model derived from neutron and X-ray total scattering via the reverse Monte Carlo method, we reveal the existence of sub-nanometer clusters of Bi, Na, and Ba. These clusters harbor distinct polar displacements, validating predictions from density functional theory calculations. This local architecture fosters a polar state characterized by miniature clusters and significant fluctuations in both the magnitude and direction of unit-cell polar vectors. Macroscopically, these features translate to enhanced high-field polarizability, suppressed hysteresis, and elevated breakdown strength. Notably, this optimized polar structure leads to a unique field-enhanced efficiency, exceptional thermal and fatigue stability, and superior discharge capacity. This work underscores how hidden local chemical ordering profoundly influences polarization dynamics and offers a promising pathway for designing next-generation dielectric energy storage materials.
